summary: The live SKILL discovery cache already invalidates on a tuple of (path, mtime_ns, size) for every discovered SKILL.md, so add/remove/rename/content edits naturally invalidate discovery records without a separate watcher.

The compiled skill graph is cached process-wide after first load and is reused without a prompt-time freshness check, which means graph boosts can lag behind live discovery until an explicit reload or process restart.

health_check already compares live discovered skill names against the compiled graph inventory and downgrades health when either side is missing skills, exposing missing_in_graph and missing_in_discovery as explicit migration diagnostics.

Because the discovery signature includes path and the compiler requires graph-metadata.skill_id to match the folder name, renaming a skill is structurally equivalent to removing one identity and adding another, even if the content is otherwise unchanged.

The checked-in hot path reads name, description, keywords, intent_signals, derived trigger phrases, and conflicts, but not importance_tier, so same-name importance-tier changes are freshness-relevant while remaining routing-neutral under the current scorer.

Any reusable advisor brief must store both the global source signature and per-skill fingerprints for every referenced skill, and the hook must restat those files before reuse; any missing file or fingerprint mismatch invalidates the cached brief immediately.

If a cached brief references a deleted skill, the hook must attempt one fresh recompute against live discovery; if no threshold-passing replacement exists, it must emit no model-visible brief, and if recompute fails it must fail open with brief null.

Rename events are treated as delete-plus-add with no silent alias migration, while same-name metadata edits always refresh the cache record and generation timestamp; routing-relevant metadata may change the brief, but routing-neutral metadata like importance_tier may legitimately re-emit identical text after recompute.

Keeping a stale cached skill label alive by aliasing it to a new or deleted identity was ruled out because it would make prompt-time output disagree with both live discovery and inventory-parity diagnostics.
